comments.body: Tagged with Java-9, where the codePoints API was introduced in the JDK.
@Naman codePoints() was introduced in Java 8. Either way, at what point do we stop tagging new versions? Do generics questions need to be tagged with java-5?

To clarify: Unicode code point support has been added over the years across versions. StringBuilder::appendCodePoint & String::codePointAt in Java 5, CharSequence::codePoints in Java 8, and String:codePoints in Java 9. I agree with shmosel that there comes a point when tagging with Java version is superfluous. So it seems here. A topic for meta.StackOverflow.com.

@shmosel Oh, I went in with the String specific implementation as documented here and tagged the question. Missed the fact the CharSequence already had it. Not sure what the community in general practices. I follow the aspect that I would edit a question with java version tag if it contains APIs specific to that version such that people reading the question are not surprised by the use of such APIs. Does it make sense to revert it now? I do agree, that the question wasn't specific to Java-9.
I find it odd that there isn't something to handle this for you
@hat Agreed. If I ever catch Brian Goetz at a conference, I'll suggest he launch a Project Papyrus to revamp Java’s text-handling and Unicode support.
@hat  Your Comment inspired me to write some utility methods to ease the work. Thank you.
@shmosel Whoops, I just realized this Answer was originally authored by you rather than me. I moved all the utility methods I wrote to another Answer so as to not morph yours.

answers.body:
IntStream::collect with StringBuilder

Use IntStream::collect with a StringBuilder.

String output = 
    "input_goes_here"
    .codePoints()                            // Generates an `IntStream` of Unicode code points, one `Integer` for each character in the string.
    .collect(                                // Collect the results of processing each code point.
        StringBuilder::new,                  // Supplier<R> supplier
        StringBuilder::appendCodePoint,      // ObjIntConsumer<R> accumulator
        StringBuilder::append                // BiConsumer<R,​R> combiner
    )                                        
    .toString()
;

If you prefer the more general CharSequence interface over concrete String, simply drop the toString() at the end. The returned StringBuilder is a CharSequence.

IntStream codePointStream = "input_goes_here".codePoints ();
CharSequence output = codePointStream.collect ( StringBuilder :: new , StringBuilder :: appendCodePoint , StringBuilder :: append );

or more direct to String by using an array passed to new String(…)

IntStream intStream = "input_goes_here".codePoints();

int[] arr;
String output = new String( (arr = intStream.toArray()), 0, arr.length );


and here's the original short solution without the superfluous IntStream intStream assignment:

int[] arr;
String output = new String( (arr = "input_goes_here".codePoints().toArray()), 0, arr.length );

not to forget the Java IO library:
use IntStream::collect with a StringWriter

String output = 
    "input_goes_here".codePoints() // Generates an IntStream of Unicode code points,
                                   //  one Integer for each character in the string.
    .collect(                      // Collect the results of processing each code point.
        StringWriter::new,         // Supplier<R> supplier
        StringWriter::write,       // ObjIntConsumer<R> accumulator
        (w1, w2) -> w1.write(      // BiConsumer<R,R> combiner
            w2.toString() ) )
    .toString();

The Answer by shmosel is correct and smart. For convenience, here are some utility methods based on the code seen there. You may want to copy-paste into your project.

Utility methods

These utility methods cover:

  • Get string from IntStream of codepoints.
  • Get string from List < Integer > of codepoints.
  • Get string from int array of code points.
package work.basil.example.text;

import java.util.Arrays;
import java.util.List;
import java.util.stream.IntStream;

public class CodePointUtils
{
    public static String from ( final IntStream codePoints )
    {
        return
                codePoints
                        .collect(                                    // Collect the code points.
                                StringBuilder :: new ,               // Supplier<R> supplier
                                StringBuilder :: appendCodePoint ,   // ObjIntConsumer<R> accumulator
                                StringBuilder :: append              // BiConsumer<R,​R> combiner
                        )
                        .toString();
    }

    public static String from ( final List < Integer > codePoints )
    {
        return
                CodePointUtils.from(
                        codePoints
                                .stream()
                                .mapToInt( Integer :: intValue )  // Returns IntStream. Pass to sibling method.
                );
    }

    public static String from ( final int[] codePoints )
    {
        return
                CodePointUtils.from(
                        Arrays.stream( codePoints )    // Returns IntStream. Pass to sibling method.
                );
    }
}

Or perhaps it would be better to wrap the exception thrown for invalid codepoints. And add a length method to replace String#length. And do some argument validation. And add nullity annotations with JSpecify. And write some introductory comments.

package work.basil.example.text;

import org.jspecify.annotations.NonNull;

import java.util.Arrays;
import java.util.List;
import java.util.Objects;
import java.util.stream.IntStream;

// Code points are the integers used to identify Unicode characters.
// Each of the 159,801 characters defined in Unicode are assigned a
// number in the range from 0 to 1,114,111 (U+0000–U+10FFFF).
// https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unicode
// https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Code_point
// Use code points rather than the essentially broken `char` type.
public class CodePointUtils
{
    // `String#length` is based on `char` and is therefore essentially broken.
    // For example: "😷".length() returns 2 rather than 1.
    public static int length ( final @NonNull String string )
    {
        Objects.requireNonNull( string );
        return string.codePoints().count();
    }

    public static @NonNull String from ( final @NonNull IntStream codePoints )
    {
        Objects.requireNonNull( codePoints );
        try
        {
            return
                    Objects.requireNonNull(
                            codePoints
                                    .collect(                                    // Collect the code points.
                                            StringBuilder :: new ,               // Supplier<R> supplier
                                            StringBuilder :: appendCodePoint ,   // ObjIntConsumer<R> accumulator
                                            StringBuilder :: append              // BiConsumer<R,​R> combiner
                                    )
                                    .toString()
                    );
        }
        catch ( IllegalArgumentException e )
        {
            throw new IllegalArgumentException( "Invalid code point(s)." );
        }
    }

    public static @NonNull String from ( final @NonNull List < Integer > codePoints )
    {
        // Make a stream from the list, pass to first sibling method.
        Objects.requireNonNull( codePoints );
        try
        {
            return
                    Objects.requireNonNull(
                            CodePointUtils.from(
                                    codePoints
                                            .stream()
                                            .mapToInt( Integer :: intValue )  // Returns IntStream. Pass to sibling method.
                            )
                    );
        }
        catch ( IllegalArgumentException e )
        {
            throw new IllegalArgumentException( "Invalid code point(s)." );
        }
    }

    public static @NonNull String from ( final @NonNull int[] codePoints )
    {
        // Make a stream from the array, pass to first sibling method.
        Objects.requireNonNull( codePoints );
        try
        {
            return
                    Objects.requireNonNull(
                            CodePointUtils.from(
                                    Arrays.stream( codePoints )    // Returns IntStream. Pass to sibling method.
                            )
                    );
        }
        catch ( IllegalArgumentException e )
        {
            throw new IllegalArgumentException( "Invalid code point(s)." );
        }
    }
}

I have not tested this code thoroughly, nor have I yet used this in production. So review carefully, and use at your own risk.

Here are some Jupiter tests to verify, and to show example usage.

package work.basil.example.text;

import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test;

import java.util.List;
import java.util.stream.IntStream;

import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.assertEquals;
import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.assertThrows;

class CodePointUtilsTest
{
    @Test
    void shouldReturnLengthInCodePoints ( )
    {
        assertEquals( 1 , CodePointUtils.length( "😷" ) );  // `String#length` reports 2 rather than 1. 
        assertEquals( 3 , CodePointUtils.length( "abc" ) );
        assertEquals( 3 , CodePointUtils.length( "123" ) );
        assertEquals( 9 , CodePointUtils.length( "👉🏽abc123😷" ) ); // First emoji has a modifier, so = 2. Last emoji = 1. Total 9.
    }

    @Test
    void shouldGetStringFromIntStream ( )
    {
        final String input = "👉🏽abc123😷";
        assertEquals( input , CodePointUtils.from( input.codePoints() ) );
    }

    @Test
    void shouldGetStringFromListInteger ( )
    {
        final String input = "👉🏽abc123😷";
        List < Integer > codePoints = input.codePoints().boxed().toList();
        assertEquals( input , CodePointUtils.from( codePoints ) );
    }

    @Test
    void shouldGetStringFromIntArray ( )
    {
        final String input = "👉🏽abc123😷";
        int[] codePoints = input.codePoints().toArray();
        assertEquals( input , CodePointUtils.from( codePoints ) );
    }

    @Test
    void shouldReturnEmptyStringForNoCodepoints ( )
    {
        final String input = "";
        int[] codePoints = input.codePoints().toArray();
        assertEquals( input , CodePointUtils.from( codePoints ) );
    }

    @Test
    void shouldNotGetStringFromFaultyInput ( )
    {
        IntStream stream = IntStream.rangeClosed( Character.MAX_CODE_POINT , Character.MAX_CODE_POINT + 1 );
        assertThrows( IllegalArgumentException.class , ( ) -> { CodePointUtils.from( stream ); } );
    }

    @Test
    void shouldRefuseNullArgument ( )
    {
        IntStream stream = null;  // Nulls not allowed by our API.
        assertThrows( NullPointerException.class , ( ) -> { CodePointUtils.from( stream ); } );
    }
}

The code points can be converted to String and the resulting Strings joined:

String output = "input_goes_here 😷"
                    .codePoints()
                    .mapToObj(Character::toString)
                    .collect(Collectors.joining());

Note: using Character#toString(int), not to be confused with Character#toString(char).
This solution is probably more expensive than already suggested ones since it creates one String for each code point.

title: Make a string from an IntStream of code point numbers?
body:
If I am working with Java streams, and end up with an IntStream of code point numbers for Unicode characters, how can I render a CharSequence such as a String? 

String output = "input_goes_here".codePoints(). ??? ;

I have found a codePoints() method on several interfaces & classes that all generate an IntStream of code points. Yet I have not been able to find any constructor or factory method that accepts the same.

  • CharSequence::codePoints() → IntStream
  • String::codePoints() → IntStream
  • StringBuilder::codePoints() → IntStream

I am looking for the converse:

➥ How to instantiate a String or CharSequence or such from an IntStream of code points?
